description: International space-law precheck rules (UN treaties + IADC guideline). Research-grade snapshot; primary sources only; not legal advice.

rules.title: National authorization and continuing supervision of the mission
rules.authority: States Parties to the Outer Space Treaty
rules.source_reference: Outer Space Treaty, Article VI (610 UNTS 205)
rules.finding_if_fail: No national authorization / continuing-supervision evidence is declared for a flight mission (OST Art. VI).
rules.limitations: Precheck only: verifies that authorization evidence is DECLARED, not that a valid authorization exists or covers this activity. Which state is the 'appropriate State Party' for a multi-state mission requires legal review.

rules.title: Registry-state jurisdiction and control: owner consent for active interference
rules.authority: States Parties to the Outer Space Treaty
rules.source_reference: Outer Space Treaty, Article VIII (610 UNTS 205)
rules.finding_if_fail: Active interference with a space object is planned without an affirmative declaration of the owner/registry state's consent (OST Art. VIII allocates jurisdiction and control to the State of registry). Absence of an affirmative 'true' is treated as no consent.
rules.limitations: For research profiles, consent 'true' is a declared scenario assumption (ADSC spec D9), never a legal fact, and the finding notes must be read that way. The precise consent instrument (agreement, contract, note verbale) required in a real case needs legal review.

rules.title: Registration of the servicer space object
rules.authority: States Parties to the Registration Convention
rules.source_reference: Convention on Registration of Objects Launched into Outer Space, Article II (1023 UNTS 15)
rules.finding_if_fail: No registration evidence (national registry entry / UN notification) is declared for a flight mission (Registration Convention Art. II).
rules.limitations: Precheck of declared evidence only. Registration timing ('as soon as practicable') and the choice of registry state among joint launching states require legal review.

rules.title: Third-party liability risk allocation evidence
rules.authority: States Parties to the Liability Convention
rules.source_reference: Convention on International Liability for Damage Caused by Space Objects, Articles II-III (961 UNTS 187)
rules.finding_if_fail: No third-party liability arrangement (insurance / indemnification / documented self-insurance) is declared. The Liability Convention makes the launching state absolutely liable for surface damage (Art. II) and fault-liable in space (Art. III); operators are normally required to carry the corresponding arrangements.
rules.limitations: The convention binds STATES, not operators; operator-level insurance obligations come from national law and vary by jurisdiction. Amounts and scope require legal review.

rules.title: Post-mission disposal lifetime within 25 years (IADC guideline)
rules.source_reference: IADC Space Debris Mitigation Guidelines, IADC-02-01, guideline 5.3.2 (objects passing through the LEO protected region)
rules.source_date_or_version: IADC-02-01 Rev. 2 (March 2020); later revisions exist - confirm the current one
rules.finding_if_fail: Design post-mission disposal lifetime exceeds the IADC 25-year guideline for the LEO protected region.
rules.limitations: Non-binding guideline. Stricter national rules supersede it where they apply - notably the US FCC 5-year rule (see FCC-ODM-5YR); under a US FCC-jurisdiction scenario the 25-year figure is NOT the operative standard. Confirm the current IADC revision before citing.
